{{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * [http://www.gensetsu.com/020309sisiyotuka/doc1.htm Shishiyotsuka Kofun (Site Report)] * [http://obito1.web.fc2.com/sisio.html Osaka Prefecture Kanan Town - Shishiyotsuka Kofun (Historical Research)] {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * [[ja:シシヨツカ古墳]]

{{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * [http://www.gensetsu.com/020309sisiyotuka/doc1.htm Shishiyotsuka Kofun (Site Report)] * [http://obito1.web.fc2.com/sisio.html Osaka Prefecture Kanan Town - Shishiyotsuka Kofun (Historical Research)] revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * [http://www.gensetsu.com/020309sisiyotuka/doc1.htm Shishiyotsuka Kofun (Site Report)] * [http://obito1.web.fc2.com/sisio.html Osaka Prefecture Kanan Town - Shishiyotsuka Kofun (Historical Research)] location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar applied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and approximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, approximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, pp. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} * {{ill|Kanayama Kofun|ja|金山古墳}} * {{ill|Kannonzuka Kofun (Habikino)|ja|観音塚古墳 (羽曳野市)}} == External Links == * The passage, aqqroximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, qq. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, qq. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar aqqlied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and aqqroximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, aqqroximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, qq. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, qq. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related {{Infobox ancient site |name= Shishiyotsuka Kofun |image=[[File:Shishiyotsuka Kofun, gaikan.jpg|280px]]
location vicinity |所在地= Kanan, Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates={{coordinates|34|29|29|N|135|38|53|E|scale:20000}} |形状= Square burial mound |規模=34.5m east-west, 25.5m north-south |築造年代= Late 6th century |埋葬施設= Cut-stone horizontal stone chamber |被葬者= |出土品= Inlaid sword fittings, lamellar armor, saddle fittings |史跡指定= |特記事項= }} '''Shishiyotsuka Kofun''' (シシヨツカ古墳) is a late {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} {{ill|square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|ja|河南町}}, Minamikawachi District, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. Its existence was confirmed through preliminary surveys related to farmland development, followed by excavation. Together with nearby mounds such as {{ill|Tsukamari Kofun|ja|ツカマリ古墳}} and Akahage Kofun, it forms part of the Hiraiishi Kofun Group from the terminal Kofun period. == Overview of Excavation == In preparation for agricultural land redevelopment in the Kogose area of Minamikawachi, the Osaka Prefecture Board of Education began trial excavations in 2001. A total of 111 test pits (1m × 2m each) were dug across the area. Test pit No. 62, located in terraced rice fields, revealed two ceiling stones and stratified soil layers at 1.25m below the surface, confirming the presence of a burial mound. named Shishiyotsuka Kofun after the local place name, full-scale excavations began in 2002. These investigations uncovered the mound, stone chamber, and surrounding moat, revealing the structure's near-complete form despite being buried under terraced rice fields. === Mound Size and Structure === The mound is a three-tiered, elongated square structure with rammed earth layers on the second and third tiers. Its height from the flat base to the remaining mound summit is 4.7 meters. The first tier measures 25.5m north-south and 34m east-west. Stone facing was identified on each tier, though parts were disturbed. The stones were set with clay mortar aqqlied to the slopes beforehand. The surrounding moat measured 8.0m wide on the north side, 5.5m on the south, 8.5m on the west, and aqqroximately 9.5m on the east, though flood damage obscured its exact width. === Stone Chamber Structure === The burial facility comprises a cut-stone horizontal stone chamber with a horizontal entrance-style stone coffin forming the rear chamber. The chamber system includes a rear chamber, an antechamber, and a passage, with a total length of 12m. The rear chamber walls are constructed of cut granite, measuring 2.47m on the right wall, 2.6m on the left, and 1.09–1.12m in width. The antechamber measures 2.47m on the right and 2.46m on the left, with a width of 1.36m. The rear chamber's ceiling stone is a single piece measuring 2.47m in length, with smooth internal surfaces. The passage, aqqroximately 6m long and 1.5–1.6m wide, is constructed from stacked natural stones without cut-stone finishes. === Artifacts === Artifacts found in the stone chamber were heavily disturbed and mostly fragmentary: 1. '''Ornaments''': Silver beads, necklaces, gilt-bronze rings, gold threads, and glass beads (1,067 pieces in total). 2. '''Weapons''': Fragments of iron swords, daggers, and arrowheads, including inlaid sword fittings with phoenix motifs, scabbard ornaments, and other fragments suggesting sophisticated craftsmanship. 3. '''Armor''': Lamellar armor fragments sufficient to reconstruct four suits of armor. 4. '''Horse Gear''': Saddle fittings, decorative plates, and other components, primarily in fragments. 5. '''Coffin Materials''': Lacquered wood coffin fragments. 6. '''Pottery''': {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} jars and pedestal bowls found in the passage, dating to the late 6th century. These suggest that the Shishiyotsuka Kofun may be one of the oldest terminal-period kofun with cut-stone chambers.Masumoto & Morikawa, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 82-170 == Likely Builders == Shishiyotsuka Kofun, along with Tsukamari and Akahage Kofun in Hiraiishi Valley, are large-scale kofun from the terminal period. No records or oral traditions identify the builders or interred individuals. However, the scale and construction suggest prominent historical figures or clans. One hypothesis links the tombs to the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, who were influential in military and diplomatic affairs in this region. Another theory considers the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, though their burial mounds are concentrated in the {{ill|Shimanokawachi Kofun Group|ja|磯長谷古墳群}}, located 3km north.Kanbayashi, "Kofungun" (2009), p. 398 == References == * Masumoto, Tetsu & Morikawa, Yusuke, "Shishiyotsuka Kofun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, qq. 82-170. * Kanbayashi, Shirō, "The Buried Persons of the Hiraiishi Kofungun," in *Kanō Kofungun and Hiraiishi Kofungun*, Osaka Prefecture Board of Education, 2009, qq. 398-421. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == Related
